title: Are stock options halal in Islam?
stanceSummary: Stock options are impermissible (haram) according to the vast majority of respected Islamic finance bodies, including the OIC Fiqh Academy, the European Fatwa Council, and AAOIFI. The core issues are gharar (excessive uncertainty) and an element of qimar (gambling), as both parties are essentially betting against each other in a zero-sum transaction. This is in contrast to purchasing stocks directly, where the investor becomes a part-owner of the company.
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

- **Stock options are haram (impermissible)** according to the vast majority of modern scholars and Islamic finance bodies
- The ruling is supported by the **OIC Fiqh Academy**, the **European Fatwa Council**, and **AAOIFI**
- The two main problems are **gharar** (excessive uncertainty) and **qimar** (gambling)
- Options are a zero-sum game where one party wins and the other loses
- This is contrasted with buying stocks directly, which makes you a part-owner of a company with shared risk

In a [2021 video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ayZYckEgx3U), Yasir Qadhi addressed this question as part of a rapid Q&A session.

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### What Are Stock Options?

A brother from Bangladesh emails and asks: are stock options halal?

For those who are not aware, an **option** is a contract between two parties that gives the buyer the right to buy or sell a security at a predetermined price on or before a predetermined date. It gives you the right, but you don't have to exercise it — it gives you the *option*, but it is not obligatory.

### The Core Problem

In essence, what happens is that two people are basically **gambling against each other**. One party hopes that the price will go up, and the other party hopes that the price will go down. They are betting against each other — that's the only reason why the two would enter into this contract.

### The Scholarly Consensus

Because of this, the **vast majority of modern scholars** have ruled that options are **impermissible (haram)** under the Sharia. This includes:

- The **Fiqh Academy Council** of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation (OIC)
- The **European Fatwa Council**
- **AAOIFI** (Accounting and Auditing Organization for Islamic Financial Institutions)
- And many other universally respected scholars in the field

This is the default position of pretty much everybody who is respected in this field.

### Why Options Are Haram

There are multiple problems involved with this contract. Most significantly:

1. **Gharar (uncertainty)**: There is an impermissible level of uncertainty — you do not know exactly what you are getting
2. **Qimar (gambling)**: Both parties are expecting the other to lose. It is a **zero-sum game** where one party takes all and the other goes into a loss

### Contrast With Direct Stock Ownership

This is in contrast to the one who purchases stock directly. The one who purchases stock is a **part-owner** of the company. He wants the company to flourish and expects the stock to go up. He doesn't know the future, but the point is he's a part-owner — if the company goes up, alhamdulillah, so does his profit. If it goes down, that's the collective loss they all share. He's not betting against another person, and there's no uncertainty that would be haram.

Therefore, the majority position — the default position — is that **stock options are not allowed** because there is an impermissible level of Islamic uncertainty, a level of gambling. And Allah knows best.
